statement.p: Scientists are conducting an experiment with a gas in a sealed container. The mass of the gas is measured, and the scientists realize that the gas is leaking over time in a linear way. Each minute, they lose 5.4 grams. Nine minutes since the experiment started, the remaining gas had a mass of 199.8 grams.

Let x be the number of minutes that have passed since the experiment started, and let y be the mass of the gas in grams at that moment. Use a linear equation to model the weight of the gas over time.
A point-slope equation to model this is .
31 minutes after the experiment started, there would be grams of gas left.
If a linear model continues to be accurate, minutes since the experiment started, all gas in the container will be gone.

li.p: A lines equation in point-slope form looks like y=m(x-x_0)+y_0, where m is the slope, and (x_0,y_0) is some point we know to be on the line. We first need to find the lines slope. Since the gas is leaking at a rate of 5.4 grams per minute, we know that the slope of the linear model is -5.4.
So now we have that y=-5.4(x-x_0)+y_0. We can use (x_0,y_0)=(9,199.8) to get {y = -5.4\mathopen{}\left(x-9\right)+199.8}.
After 31 minutes, we can find the mass of the remaining gas if we substitute 31 in for x in the equation y=-5.4(x-9)+199.8, and we have:
So after 31 minutes, there are 81 grams of gas remaining.
To find when the gas will be all gone, we need to find when there are 0 grams of gas left. We can substitute 0 in for y in the equation y=-5.4(x-9)+199.8, and we have:
So after 46 minutes, the gas will be all gone.
